name: Cloud Service and Deployment Models
description: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and deployment models including public, private, hybrid, and community cloud
key_facts: IaaS provides infrastructure (VMs, storage, networks); customer manages OS and up
PaaS provides a platform for application development; provider manages infrastructure and runtime
SaaS delivers complete applications over the internet; provider manages everything
XaaS (Anything as a Service) includes DaaS (Desktop), FaaS (Function), DBaaS (Database)
Multitenancy allows multiple customers to share the same physical infrastructure securely
common_misconceptions: PaaS and SaaS are the same (PaaS is for developers to build apps; SaaS is finished software for end users)
Private cloud is always on-premises (private cloud can be hosted by a third party)

name: High Availability and Scalability Design
description: Designing cloud architectures for high availability, fault tolerance, and scalability
key_facts: Horizontal scaling adds more instances; vertical scaling increases resources on existing instances
Auto-scaling adjusts capacity based on demand using metrics and policies
Load balancers distribute traffic across instances; support health checks for fault tolerance
Availability zones provide redundancy within a region; regions provide geographic redundancy
SLA (Service Level Agreement) defines uptime guarantees: 99.9% = 8.76 hours downtime/year
common_misconceptions: High availability and disaster recovery are the same (HA minimizes downtime; DR recovers from catastrophic failure)
Vertical scaling has no limits (there are hardware limits to how large a single instance can be)

name: Virtualization and Compute Deployment
description: Hypervisor types, VM lifecycle management, and container deployment in cloud environments
key_facts: Type 1 hypervisors (bare-metal): VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM
VM templates and golden images standardize deployments across environments
Containers use OS-level virtualization and share the host kernel for efficiency
Container orchestration (Kubernetes) manages container lifecycle, scaling, and networking
Serverless computing (FaaS) runs code without managing any infrastructure
common_misconceptions: Containers replace VMs entirely (VMs provide stronger isolation; both have valid use cases)
Serverless means no servers exist (servers still run but are fully managed by the provider)

name: Cloud Storage Types and Tiers
description: Block, object, and file storage in cloud environments, including storage tiers and lifecycle policies
key_facts: Block storage provides raw volumes attached to VMs (like virtual hard drives)
Object storage stores unstructured data as objects with metadata (S3-compatible)
File storage provides shared file systems using NFS or SMB/CIFS protocols
Storage tiers: hot (frequent access), warm (infrequent), cold (archive/rare access)
Lifecycle policies automatically move data between tiers based on age or access patterns
common_misconceptions: Object storage can be mounted like a file system (object storage uses APIs, not mount points)
All storage types perform equally (block storage has lowest latency; object storage scales best)

name: Cloud Monitoring and Optimization
description: Monitoring cloud resources, performance optimization, and capacity planning
key_facts: Key metrics: CPU utilization, memory usage, disk I/O, network throughput, latency
Alerting thresholds trigger notifications when metrics exceed defined limits
Right-sizing matches instance types to actual workload requirements to reduce cost
Reserved instances and savings plans provide discounts for committed usage
Tagging resources enables cost allocation and management by department, project, or environment
common_misconceptions: Cloud costs are always predictable (without monitoring and governance, costs can grow unexpectedly)
The largest instance type gives the best performance (right-sizing provides optimal cost-performance ratio)

name: Cloud Identity and Access Security
description: Identity management, access controls, and data protection in cloud environments
key_facts: Federated identity allows single sign-on across multiple cloud providers using SAML or OIDC
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions based on job function
Encryption at rest protects stored data; encryption in transit protects data during transfer
Key management services (KMS) centrally manage encryption keys and policies
Secrets management stores and rotates credentials, API keys, and certificates securely
common_misconceptions: Cloud provider encryption protects data from the provider itself (customer-managed keys provide additional control)
IAM policies alone are sufficient security (network controls, encryption, and monitoring are also needed)

name: Cloud Compliance and Data Sovereignty
description: Regulatory compliance, data sovereignty requirements, and audit processes in cloud
key_facts: Data sovereignty requires data to remain within specific geographic borders
Shared responsibility model divides security duties between provider and customer
Cloud compliance certifications include SOC 2, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, HIPAA
Data classification categorizes data by sensitivity: public, internal, confidential, restricted
Audit trails and logging provide evidence of compliance for regulators
common_misconceptions: Cloud provider compliance certifies the customer's deployment (customers must ensure their own workloads comply)
Data in the cloud is always accessible from anywhere (data sovereignty and regulations may restrict access)

name: DevOps and CI/CD Fundamentals
description: DevOps principles, CI/CD pipelines, and infrastructure as code
key_facts: CI (Continuous Integration) automatically builds and tests code on every commit
CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) automates release to staging or production
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) manages infrastructure through definition files (Terraform, CloudFormation)
Version control (Git) tracks all infrastructure and application code changes
Configuration management tools (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) automate server configuration
common_misconceptions: DevOps is just automation (DevOps is a culture of collaboration between development and operations)
Continuous delivery and continuous deployment are the same (delivery requires manual approval; deployment is fully automated)

name: Cloud Networking
description: Virtual networks, DNS, load balancing, and network connectivity in cloud environments
key_facts: Virtual networks (VPCs/VNets) provide isolated network segments in the cloud
Subnets divide virtual networks; security groups and NACLs control traffic
VPN and dedicated connections (ExpressRoute, Direct Connect) provide secure hybrid connectivity
CDN (Content Delivery Network) caches content at edge locations to reduce latency
DNS services provide domain resolution with health checks and failover routing
common_misconceptions: Cloud virtual networks are automatically connected to the internet (explicit configuration is needed for internet access)
All cloud network traffic is encrypted by default (encryption in transit must be configured)

name: Cloud Troubleshooting and Migration
description: Troubleshooting cloud deployment issues, performance problems, and migration strategies
key_facts: Migration strategies (6 Rs): Rehost, Replatform, Repurchase, Refactor, Retire, Retain
Common issues: connectivity failures, permission errors, resource limits, configuration drift
Performance troubleshooting: check CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network metrics
Automation drift occurs when manual changes diverge from IaC-defined state
Runbooks document step-by-step procedures for resolving common operational issues
common_misconceptions: Lift-and-shift migration optimizes for the cloud (rehosting preserves existing architecture without cloud-native optimization)
Cloud eliminates all performance problems (misconfiguration and under-provisioning cause performance issues in the cloud)
